rationale: The citing paper uses Thompson and Norton (2011) for a specific conceptual role: to support the claim that choosing feature-rich products can yield social benefits. This citation is used alongside feature-fatigue work to justify why the authors do not set a directional hypothesis for the feature-richness-to-satisfaction link and instead test it empirically. Although it is not central to the CvSS mechanism or methods, it meaningfully informs the paper's theoretical framing of possible outcomes.
relation_description: The citing paper draws on the cited paper as supporting evidence that feature-rich products may provide social utility. It uses this point to frame competing expectations about whether higher feature richness should increase or decrease satisfaction in its customization context.
